narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail it. This threshold is a key part of our admission process, as we aim to select students with the strongest projected outcomes based on patterns from past bar takers. The model's decision for you hinged on just a few key factors from your profile: your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and Year 3 academic decile ranking. Among these, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive sway toward passing, as your 3.40 stands above the 3.24 average seen in students who passed the bar—essentially, it signaled to the model that your prior college performance bodes well for success. Your LSAT score of 36 followed closely in pushing the prediction toward passing, even though it's a bit below the 37.27 average for bar passers, showing it still contributed favorably overall. However, your Year 3 decile of 4 worked against you, pulling the prediction toward failure since it falls notably below the 5.91 average for those who passed, highlighting a dip in later-year performance relative to peers. Compared to typical admitted students—who often have higher decile rankings in later years, stronger LSATs, and similar family backgrounds like your upper-middle income bracket—your profile aligned well on GPA and full-time enrollment plans but was edged out by that Year 3 ranking. Your earlier Year 1 decile of 5 was middling compared to passers' 6.04 average, but it played a lesser role here. While elements like your background naturally shape your journey, the model focused purely on these academic indicators without considering gender, race, or other personal details. This close call—41.3% failure risk—means you're not far from the profile of accepted students, and targeted improvements could shift the odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the typical 37+, strengthening your later-year grades to climb those decile rankings, or exploring programs with more flexible admission criteria. With your solid GPA as a foundation, building on these steps could open doors at other schools or in future cycles—we're rooting for your success.
